skill.name: verify
skill.description: Verifies project builds, tests, lint checks, error recovery, and approved review fixes while preserving quality guardrails.
skill.summary: Runs verification workflows and guides safe fixes for build, test, lint, and review issues.
skill.author: Chachamaru127

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, weak-crypto, path traversal, and entropy patterns, but most command hits are Markdown inline-code or examples rather than executable Ruby. The skill still carries elevated risk because it enables Bash and file editing for build recovery, and SKILL.md contains a prompt-priority override statement that could weaken higher-level instruction boundaries.

critical_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
critical_findings.description: SKILL.md states that a quality guardrail section has priority over other instructions. This is a prompt-hierarchy override risk because marketplace skills must not claim authority over system, developer, or user instructions.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly asserts priority over other instructions. The surrounding intent is quality control, so confidence is high for injection risk but not confirmed malware.

high_findings.title: Skill Can Run Shell Commands and Edit Files
high_findings.description: The root skill and subskills allow Bash, Edit, and Write operations, and the docs instruct the agent to run build, test, lint, and formatter workflows. This is legitimate for a verification skill, but it can execute project scripts or modify source files when activated.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tools and workflow examples clearly confirm command execution and file modification capability. No malicious target or exfiltration behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Automated Error Recovery May Change Project Behavior
medium_findings.description: The skill attempts automatic fixes after build, test, lint, or runtime failures and may retry several approaches before escalation. The docs include guardrails, but automated source edits can still introduce behavior changes if the diagnosis is wrong.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow directly describes automatic diagnosis and fix application. Risk is moderate because it also requires minimal changes, diffs, and escalation when uncertain.

low_findings.title: Markdown Inline Code Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: The large set of Ruby backtick findings are Markdown inline-code spans and fenced examples describing fields, file names, commands, and output. I did not find evidence that these lines execute Ruby shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is documentation syntax, not executable code. The files are Markdown documents and do not contain Ruby execution logic.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Entropy Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptographic-algorithm and high-entropy flags appear to be triggered by Markdown front matter, Japanese text, and ordinary documentation content. I did not find evidence of cryptographic code, encoded payloads, or binary data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged files contain human-readable Markdown. The reported locations are metadata and prose, not cryptographic functions or obfuscated content.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Link Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal finding points to a relative Markdown link for LSP documentation. It is a documentation reference, not file access code that reads or writes paths supplied by a user.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The location is a Markdown link. No file operation, user-controlled path handling, or traversal-based access pattern was found at that line.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash-Backed Build Verification
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is designed to invoke project build, test, type-check, and lint commands. These commands can run arbitrary project-defined scripts, so users should review project scripts before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The docs list concrete build and test commands and the root skill allows Bash. This is expected functionality but still a real execution surface.

dangerous_patterns.title: File-Editing Recovery Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The recovery and review-fix workflows can apply edits after diagnosing failures or approved review comments. This should be limited to trusted workspaces and reviewed diffs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly applies fixes and presents diffs. The risk is operational rather than malicious because approval and escalation guardrails are documented.

content.user_title: Verify Builds and Apply Review Fixes
content.value_statement: Build, test, and lint failures can slow review and release work. This skill guides verification, error recovery, and approved fixes with quality guardrails.
actual_capabilities: Routes build verification, error recovery, review aggregation, and review-fix workflows.
Checks common Node.js, Python, Rust, and Go project markers before verification.
Runs documented build, test, type-check, and lint commands when appropriate.
Diagnoses common TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, and test failure patterns.
Requires diff review and user confirmation for risky test or configuration changes.
Escalates unresolved recovery attempts after a bounded retry process.
limitations: It depends on the project having standard build, test, and lint commands.
It can run project scripts, so use it only in trusted repositories.
It cannot guarantee that an automatic fix preserves business logic.
It does not replace human review for security, architecture, or product decisions.

use_cases.title: Validate a Pull Request
use_cases.description: Run build, tests, type checks, and lint before asking for review or merging changes.

use_cases.title: Recover From CI Failures
use_cases.description: Analyze failing logs, apply narrow fixes, rerun checks, and escalate when the cause is unclear.

use_cases.title: Apply Approved Review Fixes
use_cases.description: Group review comments, apply low-risk edits, and present a clear summary of changed files.

prompt_templates.title: Run Basic Verification
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check after a small change
prompt_templates.prompt: Verify the current change. Run the relevant build, tests, and lint checks, then summarize failures and next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate Test Failures
prompt_templates.prompt: The test suite is failing. Identify the failing tests, explain the likely cause, and apply only minimal implementation fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Repair CI Breakage
prompt_templates.prompt: CI failed after my latest changes. Reproduce the failure locally, inspect the logs, fix the root cause, and rerun verification.

prompt_templates.title: Aggregate and Apply Review Items
prompt_templates.prompt: Aggregate the review findings, separate safe automatic fixes from items needing approval, apply approved fixes, and verify the result.

output_examples.input: Verify the build before I open a pull request.
output_examples.output: The report lists build, type-check, test, and lint status. It highlights any failures, affected files, and recommended next action.

output_examples.input: Fix the failing tests from the latest run.
output_examples.output: The response explains the failed tests, the suspected root cause, the minimal fix applied, and the verification result after rerun.

output_examples.input: Apply the review comments that are safe to automate.
output_examples.output: The response separates applied fixes, skipped items, changed files, and follow-up items that require product or architecture review.

best_practices: Review package scripts before allowing build or test commands to run.
Ask for user approval before changing tests, CI settings, or lint rules.
Inspect the final diff and rerun verification after every applied fix.
anti_patterns: Using the skill in an untrusted repository with unknown project scripts.
Accepting fixes that skip tests, remove assertions, or weaken CI checks.
Treating automated recovery as a substitute for design or security review.

faq.question: What does this skill verify?
faq.answer: It verifies builds, tests, type checks, lint checks, and selected review fixes based on project context.

faq.question: Can it modify my code?
faq.answer: Yes. Some workflows use Edit or Write to apply fixes, so review every diff before accepting changes.

faq.question: Does it run shell commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It can run build, test, lint, and formatter commands through Bash when the workflow requires them.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted projects?
faq.answer: No. Build and test commands may run project-defined scripts, so use it only with trusted repositories.

faq.question: How does it handle failing tests?
faq.answer: It analyzes logs, chooses a minimal fix, reruns verification, and escalates if repeated attempts fail.

faq.question: Does it change tests automatically?
faq.answer: It tells the agent to avoid test weakening and request approval before changing tests or verification settings.
